root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Understanding Evidence Guide
root.summary: Front matter and chapter 1 of the Cúram 8.1.2 Understanding Evidence Guide. Includes the Note, Edition, Contents, Notices, Privacy policy, and Trademarks sections plus the full introductory chapter, “Understanding evidence.” The chapter defines evidence as client-supplied information used with rules to determine eligibility/entitlement, explains why evidence is collected, how it evolves over time, how it is shared or restricted across cases/programs, and when it must be verified. It covers evidence capture via online, paper, and IEG channels; maintenance in prospect person, person, and case records; dynamic vs static evidence screens; predefined person evidence; extending and relating evidence types; recording changes and correction/succession history; approval workflows for sensitive evidence; and one-time evidence transfer versus ongoing sharing between cases.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note for the document. It tells readers to review the Notices section on page 21 before using the information or the supported product, and shows the Merative US L.P. copyright notice (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory “Edition” pages for the Cúram evidence guide. States this edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, then begins the Contents and the first major chapter, “Understanding evidence.” The section defines evidence, explains why it is collected, how it is captured (online, paper, IEG, dynamic/static screens), where it is stored and shared across cases, how verification works, and how static vs dynamic evidence differs. It also starts the list of predefined person evidence types.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-20
children.summary: Explains core evidence concepts in Cúram: default person/prospect person evidence types, sharing with cases, dynamic evidence extension, and why person evidence must stay configured for system processes. Covers solution modules, creating new evidence types, parent-child and optional evidence relationships, and how relationships affect filtering and validations. Describes evidence change handling via succession vs correction, complete change history, approval checks for sensitive evidence, and one-time evidence transfer between cases versus ongoing evidence sharing.

Introductory “Edition” section for the Cúram evidence guide. It applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, then starts the Contents and Chapter 1, “Understanding evidence.” The chapter defines evidence, explains why it is collected, how it is captured (online, paper, IEG, dynamic/static screens), where it is stored and shared across cases, and how it is verified. It also covers predefined person evidence, extending evidence types, relationships, change handling, approval checks, and one-time transfer versus ongoing sharing.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Contains legal and licensing notices: sample data are fictitious, sample programs may be copied/modified/distributed under the Merative Client Agreement, but are provided “AS IS” with no warranty or liability. Includes a link to the Merative privacy policy, trademark notices for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and copyright years 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark and copyright/legal notices for the document. It identifies Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX trademarks, notes that other names may belong to third parties, and states the sample programs are provided “AS IS” under the Merative Client Agreement. It also includes the Merative privacy policy link and copyright years 2012, 2024.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication. Covers personal and enterprise reproduction rights, restrictions on distribution and derivative works, export-law compliance, and Merative’s right to withdraw permission. States the content is provided “AS-IS” with no warranties, no patent license granted, and third-party product/site references are informational only. Ends with copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: This contents section lists the document’s front matter and chapter 1, “Understanding evidence,” with subsections on what evidence is, who uses it, how it is captured, verified, stored, extended, related, changed, approved, and copied between cases. It also lists Note, Edition, Notices, Privacy policy, and Trademarks. Useful as a navigation index for finding evidence-related topics and referenced front-matter sections.

children.title: 1 Understanding evidence
1.1 What is evidence?
children.summary: Evidence is client-supplied information used to assess eligibility or entitlement, often grouped into logical evidence types with related attributes. This section explains that evidence is central to Cúram social programs and is used with business rules to determine outcomes. Examples include employment status, income, address, and bank account evidence, showing that evidence may affect eligibility, communication, or payment setup. It also notes that some common person/prospect person evidence types are provided by default, while customers can configure others.

children.title: 1.2 Who needs evidence and why is evidence collected?
children.summary: Explains why organizations collect evidence: to assess eligibility and financial entitlement under legislative or policy rules. Evidence must support changing values over time so benefits can be reassessed from the correct effective date. It may be shared across programs to avoid duplicate entry and keep eligibility calculations current, or restricted to one program. Some evidence must be verified, such as birth details via a birth certificate. Useful for understanding evidence purpose, evolution, sharing, and verification requirements.

children.title: 1.3 How and where is evidence captured?
children.summary: Explains how evidence is captured and maintained in Cúram. Evidence can come from online Universal Access applications, paper applications entered by caseworkers, or Intelligent Evidence Gathering (IEG) scripts. It also describes designing evidence screens via dynamic evidence or static evidence, and how IEG data maps into evidence entities. Evidence is accessed only within prospect person, person, or case type records, such as Application Case, Integrated Case, or Product Delivery, and can be shared across case types.

children.title: 1.4 How is evidence verified?
children.summary: Explains how evidence verification works in Cúram. Evidence verification checks client-provided information against sources like certificates, with requirements driven by policy or legislation and configurable by evidence type. The Verification Engine applies these rules, evaluates recorded verifications, and integrates with evidence maintenance so activation can depend on required items or groups of items. It also covers conditional verifications using CER rule sets, including cross-evidence rules, propagation configurations, and display rulesets, plus an example of verifying total income across records.

children.title: 1.5 How is evidence stored?
children.summary: Explains how evidence is persisted in Cúram 8.1.2. Static evidence uses its own database entity per evidence type, with metadata generating UI/database artifacts. Dynamic evidence stores all dynamic types in a shared runtime entity/table structure, using only two tables across types. Data captured through an IEG script is first held in a temporary data store and mapped to an evidence record only after submission. Also notes examples of dynamic evidence such as address and bank account.

children.title: 1.6 Predefined person evidence
children.summary: Describes predefined person/prospect person evidence in Cúram. Nine evidence types are configured by default and must remain on person and prospect person entities: gender, birth/death details, names, email addresses, addresses, identifications, relationships, bank accounts, and phone numbers. Explains that case evidence can be shared back to person records, supports verification and sharing, and that person evidence has no In Edit state because it is auto-activated. Also notes customization limits and extension/conversion requirements for adding attributes or migrating data.

children.title: 1.7 How are existing evidence types extended?
children.summary: Organizations extend existing evidence by either associating new dynamic evidence types with person/prospect person records or adding new attributes to existing dynamic evidence types. The section points to two main approaches: creating and associating new evidence types, and extending existing ones. It sits in a broader discussion of how evidence types relate, including parent-child relationships and their effects on filtering and validation.

children.title: 1.8 How do evidence types relate to each other?
children.summary: Explains how evidence types can relate through parent-child and parent-child-grandchild relationships. Gives examples such as asset evidence with child Asset Ownership evidence, and notes that an evidence type can be both parent and child. Describes practical effects on maintenance: child evidence lists are filtered by parent, validations can enforce relationship rules, and parents may be blocked from deletion while active children exist. Also mentions relationships can be optional, such as housing expense and loan evidence.

children.title: 1.9 How are changes to evidence recorded?
children.summary: Explains how evidence changes are recorded through two mechanisms: succession (a new effective-dated change that follows prior information) and correction (overwriting incorrect data from the original date). Describes change history tracking for person, prospect person, and case evidence, including type of change, timestamp, user, and audit/eligibility purposes. Also notes how latest activity, succession history, and correction history are displayed, including “Updated By” details.

children.title: 1.10 How are changes to evidence approved?
children.summary: Explains how evidence approval checks add a supervisor review step before evidence can be activated or removed. Covers configurable approval percentages by evidence type, automatic approval when submitter and approver are the same user, and notifications/status tracking for submitted, approved, and rejected records. Notes approvals do not apply to Person tab evidence unless also configured on Integrated Case, and can be applied at user, position, or organization unit levels.

children.title: 1.11 Can evidence be copied from one case to another?
children.summary: Explains that case evidence can be transferred from one case to another. Distinguishes evidence sharing, which keeps cases continuously synchronized, from one-time evidence transfer/copy, which copies evidence once and leaves the cases separate. Gives an example of moving a case member from an Integrated Case to a new individual case. Notes that one-time copy can include historic data and avoids re-entering information, but there is no ongoing connection between the two cases.

Introduces Cúram evidence as client-provided information used with rules to determine eligibility and entitlement. Explains why evidence is collected, how it must evolve over time, when it can be shared or restricted across programs, and when verification is required. Describes capture via online, paper, or IEG intake; where evidence is maintained; and how it is stored in static vs dynamic evidence and temporary IEG data. Also covers predefined person evidence, extending/relating evidence types, change history, approval workflows, and one-time evidence transfer between cases.
